ai.0: Everything in Unit 3 Area of Study 2 — and most of the year beyond it — grows from one question: how can an object move another object it never touches? Read this story in one sitting. Nothing here needs to be solved, only seen.
ai.1: Let go of your phone and it falls. Nothing visible reaches up to take it — no string, no hand, no puff of air — yet the instant your fingers open, something accelerates it toward the floor at 9.8 m s⁻², every time.
ai.2: A compass needle, sealed inside its plastic case, swings to the north wherever in Victoria you stand. A balloon rubbed on a jumper lifts your hair from a handsbreadth away. Three small mysteries, one shared puzzle: in each case, motion arrives with no contact at all.
ai.3: This puzzle has bothered better minds than ours. Newton wrote the law of gravitation that still guides spacecraft, yet he privately called the notion of bodies acting on one another across empty space an absurdity, and declined to guess how the trick was done. The whole of this Area of Study — its official title is How do things move without contact? — is the modern reply.
ai.4: The reply is a single idea, the field, plus a year of consequences. This page tells the story end to end, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2 it arrives in a landscape you already know.
ai.5: Connects You have been using a field strength since Year 11: the 9.8 you multiply masses by carries the unit N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram placed at that point. Pass 2 chapter 4 makes that unit the centre of Unit 3.
ai.6: The answer arrived from an unlikely direction. Michael Faraday — a bookbinder's apprentice who taught himself science between jobs — spent the 1830s watching iron filings arrange themselves into arcs around magnets. Where others saw a pretty pattern, Faraday saw something bolder: the magnet alters the space around it, and that altered condition of space — he called the region a field — is real, present whether or not anything is there to be acted on.
ai.7: The modern definition keeps his idea and tightens it: a field is a physical quantity that has a value at every point in space. Some fields store a single number at each point — a sports analyst's heat map, recording how often a player occupied each patch of the MCG, is exactly this kind of field.
ai.8: The fields in this Area of Study store something richer at every point: an arrow. The arrow's direction tells you which way the force exerted on a suitable test object placed there would act, and its length tells you how strongly. A field diagram, with its smooth lines and arrowheads, is a map of those arrows — direction along the line, strength shown by how closely the lines crowd together.
ai.9: One drawing rule does real work: field lines never cross. If two lines met, the field at that point would have two directions at once — and no force can act in two directions at once.
ai.10: Connects A field diagram is your Unit 2 force diagram scaled up: instead of drawing the arrows acting on one object, you pre-compute the arrow for every point in space. The precise definition, test objects and field-line rules open the core at What is a field?
ai.11: Unit 3 hands you three of these maps. The gravitational field is sourced by mass and acts on mass. The electric field is sourced by charge and acts on charge. The magnetic field is sourced by moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet — and acts on moving charge. Figure 1.1 draws the three side by side, and the family resemblance is the point: one grammar, three dialects.
ai.12: Now the differences, because the differences carry the physics. Mass comes in one kind only — there is no negative mass — so gravity only ever attracts, and every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in, never out.
ai.13: Electric charge comes in two signs. Field lines flow out of positive charges (sources) and into negative charges (sinks), and either kind can exist on its own — an isolated charge is called a monopole. Two signs means electric forces can attract or repel.
ai.14: Magnetism follows the strangest rule of the three. Nobody has ever found an isolated north or south pole: cut a bar magnet in half and you hold two complete magnets, each with both poles, all the way down to subatomic particles. Magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink permanently paired — which is why the magnet's lines in Figure 1.1 leave the north face and loop around to the south.
ai.15: Hold that three-way contrast — gravity: monopoles of one sign; electricity: monopoles of both signs; magnetism: dipoles only — and the three maps stop being three things to memorise and become one idea with three settings. Simulation 1.1 lets you test all three for yourself.
ai.16: Connects This comparison becomes an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles — in Comparing the three fields, and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
ai.17: Here is the first of four threads to carry through the year. Lift your schoolbag from the floor to the desk and you have done work against the gravitational field. That energy has not vanished: it is stored in the bag's raised position, and the moment the bag slides off the desk it is returned as kinetic energy, in full.
ai.18: Every field in this Area of Study runs the same bank. Separate a positive and a negative charge and you bank energy in their separation; release them and the account empties back into motion. Position in a field is stored energy — potential energy — and the whole topic is double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, total unchanged.
ai.19: The accounting unit is the detail to watch. Gravitational stores are tallied per kilogram of mass; electrical stores are tallied per coulomb of charge — and "energy per coulomb" is a quantity you have used since Unit 1 under its everyday name, voltage. One formula, W = qV not on sheet, says exactly this: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V — the formula sheet carries the same statement folded into ½mv² = qV. Nothing to calculate yet — only notice that it is Unit 1's definition of voltage read backwards.
ai.20: And when a field is not uniform, the store is read graphically, as the area under a force–distance graph — the same trick Units 1&2 used for springs. One ledger, new deposit types.
ai.21: Connects Gravitational storage is banked in Gravity and orbits and electrical storage in Electric fields. Area of Study 1 runs the identical ledger for projectiles and collisions, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect will use voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
ai.22: Thread two is a shape. Newton imagined a cannon on a mountaintop above the atmosphere, fired horizontally. A slow cannonball arcs to the ground nearby; a faster one lands further away; and at about 7.9 km s⁻¹ something remarkable happens — the ball falls toward Earth exactly as fast as Earth's surface curves away beneath it. It is falling continuously, and missing continuously: that is all an orbit is.
ai.23: The Moon is Newton's cannonball, fired long ago. In every second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — the distance its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise follow — and in that same second its sideways velocity carries it far enough that the drop never brings it closer. A satellite needs no engine to stay up; it needs sideways speed and a single inward force: gravity.
ai.24: This rewrites what you may believe about astronauts. At the International Space Station's altitude the gravitational field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — close to 90 per cent of its surface value — so "there is no gravity in space" is wrong. Astronauts float because the station and everyone inside it are falling together: no surface exerts a normal force on anyone, and the normal force is what is felt as weight. Physicists call the state apparent weightlessness, and the word "apparent" is doing honest work.
ai.25: Now the costume change. A charged particle moving across a magnetic field experiences a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet, that points at right angles to the particle's velocity — always. A sideways force of constant size changes a path's direction but never its speed, so the particle is steered into a circle, exactly as the satellite is. Whether the inward role is played by gravity on a satellite or by a magnetic field on an electron in a synchrotron ring, it is one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two costumes — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never stops changing.
ai.26: Connects Orbits are built properly in Satellites and circling charges in Circular paths. Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — learn to spot which force is playing the inward, centripetal role and half the year's hardest problems collapse into one.
ai.27: Thread three: fields are not only for explaining nature — you can build with them. Start from the workhorse fact of this Area of Study: a current-carrying wire placed across a magnetic field experiences a force, named by F = nIlB on formula sheet. Reverse the current and the force reverses too.
ai.28: Now bend the wire into a rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles. Current travels up one side of the coil and down the other, so the two sides experience equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair, and the coil rotates. There is one flaw in the design: half a turn later, the same pair of forces would turn it back the other way.
ai.29: The fix is a small piece of genius called the split-ring commutator — a copper ring cut in two that reverses the current through the coil every half-turn, so the turning effect always acts the same way. That is a DC motor, the machine inside every drill, fan and electric vehicle, and a diagram you will come to know intimately. Run the film backwards — spin the coil with wind, water or steam — and a current is driven through the wire instead: that machine is a generator, and it is the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
ai.30: The other great field machine is the particle accelerator, whose logic is a two-line summary of this Area of Study: a uniform electric field adds kinetic energy to a charge along a straight line, and a uniform magnetic field bends the charge's path without changing its speed. The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton chains the two together, holding electrons at more than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
ai.31: Connects The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask about almost every year — is built in Motors & accelerators. Area of Study 3 will run the identical hardware in reverse to light the state.
ai.32: The last thread points past the horizon of this Area of Study. Among its dot points is a small act of sorting: classify every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. It looks like housekeeping; it is the most consequential classification of the year.
ai.33: Changing magnetic fields drive currents in nearby conductors — that single fact, Faraday's discovery of induction, is how almost every power station on Earth generates electricity, and it is Area of Study 3 in one sentence. Unit 4 then takes the idea somewhere astonishing: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field, and the self-sustaining pair detaches from charges and wires altogether and travels through empty space. That travelling ripple of fields is light. The field, invented as a bookkeeping device for forces, turns out to be a thing in its own right.
ai.34: A second loose thread is printed in the study design itself: the circular-path rule for charges is guaranteed only while the speed v is much less than c. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both live outside that fine print, and Unit 4's relativity is the reading of it. A third: the practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — is a dress rehearsal for the student-designed investigation that ends Unit 4.
ai.35: Connects The accelerator endpoint of this story is examined in Pass 3. Induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now so that none of them arrives as a stranger.
ai.36: You have now read the whole Area of Study once. Everything from here is the same story at higher resolution, and the book is built so you always know which resolution you are reading.
ai.37: Pass 2 is the core, in five chapters meant to be read the night before your class meets each topic: the field idea; gravity and orbits; electric fields; magnetic fields; motors and accelerators. There you will find every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard situations, and quick checks to confirm each idea landed.
ai.38: Pass 3 is for exam season. It walks the sixteen key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — the order the exam is written from — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors' advice and exam-style questions with full solutions. Between passes, depth links like the row below carry you up and down, and this page stays one click away for whenever the detail starts to obscure the shape.
ai.39: Connects Begin Pass 2 · The field idea whenever you are ready, and bookmark Pass 3 · Fields and interactions for when the exam is real.

socratic.0:
Socrates. Let us begin the whole of this Area of Study with a single question, and survey rather than solve. Tell me: when one object moves another, what passes between them?
Student. Usually a push or a pull where they touch — a hand on a door, a bat on a ball.
Socrates. And if I told you that the next several months of your physics turn on the cases where nothing touches at all — how an object moves another without contact — would you say we had a small puzzle or a large one?
Student. A large one, I suspect, if it carries a whole year.
socratic.1:
Socrates. Then take the plainest case. You release your phone in mid-air. What reaches up to seize it?
Student. Nothing I can see — no string, no hand, not even a breath of air.
Socrates. And yet?
Student. And yet the instant I let go, it accelerates toward the floor — at 9.8 m s⁻², every single time, whatever the phone.
socratic.2:
Socrates. Now hold three observations together. A compass needle, sealed in its plastic case, swings to the north anywhere in Victoria. A balloon rubbed on your jumper lifts hair toward it from a handsbreadth away. And your falling phone. What do the three share?
Student. In each one, motion begins with nothing touching the thing that moves. The same puzzle, three times over.
socratic.3:
Socrates. You are in distinguished company in being troubled by it. Newton wrote the law of gravitation that still guides spacecraft — would you guess he was content with it?
Student. I'd assume so, since it works so well.
Socrates. Privately he thought the idea of bodies acting on one another across empty space an absurdity, and refused to say how it happened. The official title of this Area of Study is the very question — How do things move without contact? — and everything we do is the modern answer.
Student. So we are finishing an argument Newton wouldn't even start.
socratic.4:
Socrates. Just so. And the answer, when it comes, is remarkably economical. How many ideas would you spend on a year of physics?
Student. As few as I could get away with.
Socrates. Then you will be pleased: the answer is one idea — the field — together with a year of its consequences. We survey the whole thing now, end to end, so that when the detail arrives in Pass 2 it lands in country you already know.
socratic.5:
Connects Socrates. You have in fact met a field strength already. What unit hides behind multiplying 9.8 by a mass?
Student. N kg⁻¹ — newtons of force for every kilogram placed at that point. We used it in Year 11 without naming it.
Socrates. And Pass 2 chapter 4 sets that very unit at the centre of Unit 3.
socratic.6:
Socrates. The answer arrived from an unlikely man. Picture a bookbinder's apprentice, teaching himself science between jobs, watching iron filings settle into arcs around a magnet through the 1830s. Most people saw a pretty pattern. What more might one claim?
Student. That the pattern points at something — that the magnet has changed the space itself.
Socrates. Exactly Faraday's leap. He held that the magnet alters the region around it, and that this altered condition of space — which he called a field — is real, and is there whether or not anything sits in it to be pushed.
socratic.7:
Socrates. The modern definition keeps his idea and sharpens it. Could you frame a field as a kind of value spread over space?
Student. A physical quantity that has a value at every point in space — that's the shape of it.
Socrates. And must that value be elaborate? Consider a sports analyst's heat map of the MCG, recording how often a player stood on each patch of grass.
Student. That's just one number at each point. So that already counts as a field — the simplest kind.
socratic.8:
Socrates. Our fields carry something richer at each point. If a number won't do, what would you place there to capture a force?
Student. An arrow — it has a direction as well as a size.
Socrates. Then say what each part of the arrow tells you.
Student. Its direction is the direction of the force on a suitable test object placed there, and its length is the strength of that force.
Socrates. And a field diagram, with all its lines and arrowheads?
Student. A map of those arrows — direction running along each line, and strength shown by how tightly the lines are packed.
socratic.9:
Socrates. One drawing rule looks like mere tidiness but isn't. Suppose two field lines crossed. What would the field be doing at the crossing point?
Student. Pointing two ways at once — so the force there would have two directions.
Socrates. And can a force act in two directions at once?
Student. No. So field lines can never cross. The rule is forced on us.
socratic.10:
Connects Socrates. You drew force diagrams in Unit 2 — arrows on a single object. How is a field diagram its extension?
Student. Instead of drawing the arrows acting on one object, the arrow is fixed in advance for every point in space.
Socrates. The precise definition, the test objects and the line rules open the core at What is a field?
socratic.11:
Socrates. Unit 3 hands you three such maps. Let me give you each source and ask what it acts on. The gravitational field springs from mass — and acts on?
Student. On mass.
Socrates. The electric field springs from charge, and acts on?
Student. On charge.
Socrates. And the magnetic field springs from moving charge — a current in a wire, or the orbiting and spinning electrons inside a bar magnet. What does it act on?
Student. On moving charge, to match. Figure 1.1 sets all three side by side — and they rhyme.
Socrates. One grammar, then, in three dialects.
socratic.12:
Socrates. But the differences carry the real physics. Is there such a thing as negative mass?
Student. No — mass comes in one kind only.
Socrates. Then what can gravity do, and never do?
Student. It can only attract, never repel — so every gravitational field line points inward. In the language of Figure 1.1, a mass is purely a sink: lines flow in and never out.
socratic.13:
Socrates. Now charge. How many signs does it come in?
Student. Two — positive and negative.
Socrates. So draw the lines for me.
Student. They flow out of positive charges, which are sources, and into negative charges, which are sinks. And either kind can sit alone — an isolated charge is a monopole.
Socrates. And with two signs available, what becomes possible that gravity forbids?
Student. Repulsion as well as attraction — electric forces can do both.
socratic.14:
Socrates. Magnetism keeps the strangest rule. Try to obtain a lone north pole: take a bar magnet and cut it in half. What do you hold?
Student. Two complete magnets, each with its own north and south.
Socrates. Cut again, and again, down to subatomic particles?
Student. Still no lone pole has ever turned up. So magnetic sources are dipoles only — a source and a sink locked together.
Socrates. Which is why, in Figure 1.1, the magnet's lines leave the north face and loop right around to the south.
socratic.15:
Socrates. Set the three side by side. Gravity?
Student. Monopoles of one sign.
Socrates. Electricity?
Student. Monopoles of both signs.
Socrates. Magnetism?
Student. Dipoles only. So the three maps aren't three things to memorise — they're one idea with three settings.
Socrates. And Simulation 1.1 lets you test all three for yourself.
socratic.16:
Connects Socrates. This contrast hardens into an examinable table — sources, directions, shapes, attraction and repulsion, monopoles and dipoles. Where is it built?
Student. In Comparing the three fields — and the exam harvests it dot point by dot point in Pass 3.
socratic.17:
Socrates. That comparison is the first of four threads we'll follow all year. Here is the second's seed. When you lift your schoolbag from the floor to the desk, what are you working against?
Student. The gravitational field.
Socrates. And does that energy you spent simply vanish?
Student. No — it's stored in the bag's raised position. The moment it slides off the desk, it comes back as kinetic energy, in full.
socratic.18:
Socrates. Does any field here behave differently?
Student. No — they all do this. Pull a positive and a negative charge apart and you store energy in their separation; let them go and the energy returns as motion.
Socrates. So what is position in a field, in one phrase?
Student. Stored energy — potential energy. The whole topic is double-entry bookkeeping: energy out of one account, into another, the total never changing.
socratic.19:
Socrates. Mind the unit of account, for it differs by field. Gravitational stores are tallied per what?
Student. Per kilogram of mass.
Socrates. And electrical stores?
Student. Per coulomb of charge — energy per coulomb.
Socrates. A name you've used since Unit 1 hides in that phrase. Which?
Student. Voltage. And W = qV not on sheet says exactly that: the energy transformed when a charge q moves through a potential difference V.
Socrates. The formula sheet folds the same statement into ½mv² = qV — though we calculate nothing yet. Read it as what?
Student. As Unit 1's definition of voltage, run in reverse.
socratic.20:
Socrates. And when a field is not uniform, so the force keeps changing? Then a single multiplication won't tally the store. How did you read such a thing for a spring in Units 1 and 2?
Student. As the area under a force–distance graph.
Socrates. The very same method serves here. One ledger, then —
Student. — just new types of deposit.
socratic.21:
Connects Socrates. Where is each store banked in Pass 2?
Student. Gravitational storage in Gravity and orbits, and electrical storage in Electric fields.
Socrates. And the same ledger reappears — for projectiles and collisions in Area of Study 1, and in Unit 4 the photoelectric effect uses voltage as a measuring instrument for single electrons.
socratic.22:
Socrates. Thread two is not an equation but a shape. Stand Newton's cannon on a mountaintop above the air and fire it horizontally. A slow ball?
Student. Arcs to the ground nearby.
Socrates. A faster one?
Student. Lands further off.
Socrates. And at about 7.9 km s⁻¹, something remarkable. What?
Student. The ball falls toward Earth exactly as fast as Earth's surface curves away beneath it. It's falling continuously and missing continuously.
Socrates. Which is to say?
Student. That's all an orbit is.
socratic.23:
Socrates. Then what is the Moon?
Student. Newton's cannonball, fired long ago. Each second it falls about 1.4 mm toward Earth — how far its curved path drops below the straight line it would otherwise take.
Socrates. And why does that fall never bring it down?
Student. In the same second its sideways velocity carries it far enough that the drop never closes the gap.
Socrates. So what does a satellite need to stay up — an engine?
Student. No engine at all. Only sideways speed and one inward force: gravity.
socratic.24:
Socrates. This unseats a popular belief. People say there's no gravity in space — is there none at the International Space Station?
Student. There must be plenty, or it wouldn't orbit.
Socrates. At that altitude the field strength is still about 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — near 90 per cent of its surface value. So why do astronauts float?
Student. Because the station and everyone in it are falling together. No surface pushes up on anyone — and the normal force is what we feel as weight.
Socrates. So we name it carefully — apparent weightlessness. Why does "apparent" earn its place?
Student. Because the weight is still there; only the sensation of it is gone.
socratic.25:
Socrates. Now watch the same mathematics wear a different costume. A charged particle crosses a magnetic field and feels a force, named by F = qvB on formula sheet. Which way does that force point?
Student. At right angles to the particle's velocity — always.
Socrates. And a sideways force of constant size — what does it do to the path?
Student. It changes the direction but never the speed, so the particle is steered into a circle — just like the satellite.
Socrates. So whether gravity holds a satellite or a magnetic field bends an electron in a synchrotron ring?
Student. It's one piece of circular-motion mathematics in two forms — constant speed, never constant velocity, because the direction never stops changing.
socratic.26:
Connects Socrates. Where are these two built in full?
Student. Orbits in Satellites, and circling charges in Circular paths.
Socrates. And Area of Study 1 supplies the circular-motion rule both obey — so that spotting which force plays the inward, centripetal role collapses many of the year's hardest problems into one.
socratic.27:
Socrates. Thread three turns from explaining nature to building things. Start from the central fact: lay a current-carrying wire across a magnetic field. What happens?
Student. It feels a force — named by F = nIlB on formula sheet.
Socrates. And if you reverse the current?
Student. The force reverses with it.
socratic.28:
Socrates. Bend that wire into a rectangular coil on an axle between two magnet poles. Current runs up one side and down the other — so what do the two sides feel?
Student. Equal forces in opposite directions — a turning pair. The coil rotates.
Socrates. Let it turn half a way round, though. What goes wrong?
Student. The same pair of forces would now turn it back the other way. It would just rock, not spin.
socratic.29:
Socrates. So we need a trick that flips the current at exactly the right moment. Imagine a copper ring cut in two that reverses the current through the coil every half-turn. What does that buy us?
Student. The turning effect always acts the same way — that's the split-ring commutator. And the whole thing is a DC motor, the machine inside every drill, fan and electric vehicle.
Socrates. We'll draw it in detail later. Now run it backwards — spin the coil by wind, water or steam. What flows?
Student. A current is driven through the wire instead. That machine is a generator — the whole subject of Area of Study 3.
socratic.30:
Socrates. The other great field machine is the particle accelerator, and its whole operation is two lines of this Area of Study. First, what does a uniform electric field do to a charge along a straight line?
Student. It adds kinetic energy.
Socrates. And a uniform magnetic field?
Student. Bends the charge's path without changing its speed.
Socrates. Chain the two together, as the Australian Synchrotron in Clayton does, and how fast do the electrons go?
Student. More than 99.99 per cent of the speed of light.
socratic.31:
Connects Socrates. The motor — with the labelled split-ring commutator diagram examiners ask about almost every year — is built where?
Student. In Motors & accelerators.
Socrates. And Area of Study 3 runs the very same hardware in reverse to supply the state's electricity.
socratic.32:
Socrates. The last thread reaches past this Area of Study. One dot point asks a small sorting task: label every field as static or changing, and as uniform or non-uniform. Does that sound important to you?
Student. Honestly, it sounds like bookkeeping — a minor classification.
Socrates. Hold that judgement. It is the most consequential classification of the year.
socratic.33:
Socrates. Why so? Begin with a changing magnetic field near a conductor. What does it do?
Student. It drives a current in that conductor.
Socrates. That single fact is Faraday's induction — and how nearly every power station on Earth makes electricity. There is Area of Study 3 in one sentence. Now let Unit 4 extend it: a changing electric field creates a changing magnetic field, which recreates the electric field —
Student. — so the pair keeps regenerating itself, and detaches from charges and wires altogether and travels through empty space.
Socrates. And that travelling ripple of fields is?
Student. Light. So the field, brought in just to keep books on forces, turns out to be a thing in its own right.
socratic.34:
Socrates. Two loose threads remain. The study design warns that the circular-path rule for charges holds only while the speed v is much less than c. Who breaks that condition?
Student. Synchrotron electrons and GPS satellite clocks both — they sit outside it. Unit 4's relativity must deal with them.
Socrates. And the third thread, nearer to hand?
Student. The practical investigation of motor torque — varying the current, the field and the number of loops — a warm-up for the student-designed investigation that ends Unit 4.
socratic.35:
Connects Socrates. Where is the accelerator endpoint examined?
Student. In Pass 3. And induction, electromagnetic waves and relativity belong to Area of Study 3 and Unit 4 — signposted now so none of them arrives a stranger.
socratic.36:
Socrates. We have now walked the whole Area of Study once. Is there anything left to learn, or only the same things more closely?
Student. The same content at higher resolution, I'd say.
Socrates. Precisely — and the book is built so the current resolution is always plain.
socratic.37:
Socrates. Pass 2 is the core — five chapters meant for the night before each topic is taught. Can you name them?
Student. The field idea; gravity and orbits; electric fields; magnetic fields; motors and accelerators.
Socrates. And what does it carry?
Student. Every examinable equation with its formula-sheet status, the standard situations, and quick checks to confirm each idea has landed.
socratic.38:
Socrates. And Pass 3 — when is it for?
Student. Exam season. It walks the sixteen key-knowledge dot points in study-design order — the order the exam is written from — with the subtleties, the classic traps, the assessors' advice, and exam-style questions with full solutions.
Socrates. Between passes the depth links like the row below move up and down — and this page stays one click away whenever the detail begins to hide the shape of the whole.
socratic.39:
Connects Socrates. So, when you are ready, where do you go?
Student. Begin Pass 2 · The field idea — and bookmark Pass 3 · Fields and interactions for the exam period.
Socrates. Then we have surveyed the land. Let the detail come; it will find you at home in it.
